skill.name: python-database-patterns
skill.description: Provides SQLAlchemy 2.0 database patterns for Python models, async sessions, transactions, connection pools, and Alembic migrations.
skill.summary: Build modern Python database code with SQLAlchemy 2.0, async sessions, transactions, pools, and migrations.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown code fences, inline code, and SQLAlchemy examples rather than executable malware. The skill is documentation-focused and shows legitimate Bash, environment, and database configuration examples, so the remaining risk is low and publishable with normal user caution.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner treated Markdown code fences and inline code as Ruby shell backticks. These sections are static documentation examples, not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visibly Markdown code fences or tables. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution or untrusted shell execution.

low_findings.title: Documented Alembic Commands Need User Intent
low_findings.description: The migration guide includes pip and Alembic Bash commands. They are legitimate setup examples, but an agent with Bash access should run them only after user confirmation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clear and legitimate database migration commands. Risk remains only if an assistant executes them without project context or user approval.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Database Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The skill shows placeholder database URLs and reads settings or environment values. No real secrets or exfiltration paths were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use placeholder credentials and local configuration reads. There is no network call, logging of secrets, or outbound data transfer.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Substring Matches
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography alerts correspond to description text and SQLAlchemy descending sort syntax. No cryptographic function or algorithm appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows metadata text and order_by(...desc()) usage, not DES, MD5, SHA1, or encryption code. The high static finding is therefore a false positive.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are ORM Examples
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts map to ORM query, flush, and result-handling examples. No host enumeration, file discovery, or sensitive environment probing was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are database object access or SQL execution examples inside documentation. They do not inspect the operating system or collect host details.

content.value_statement: Python database code can fail through weak session handling, unsafe migrations, or poor pooling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical SQLAlchemy 2.0 patterns for reliable applications.
actual_capabilities: Shows SQLAlchemy 2.0 model definitions with mapped columns and relationships.
Provides async engine and session factory patterns for FastAPI-style applications.
Explains transaction handling with savepoints, isolation levels, and locking.
Documents Alembic setup, migration commands, and production-safe schema changes.
Covers connection pool sizing, health checks, read replicas, and shutdown handling.
Includes raw SQL examples with bound parameters for safer query construction.
limitations: Does not inspect a live database schema or run migrations by itself.
Uses illustrative placeholder database URLs that must be replaced safely.
Does not cover every database driver or vendor-specific SQL feature.
Requires users to adapt examples to their application architecture.

use_cases.title: Build Async Data Access
use_cases.description: Create async SQLAlchemy sessions, queries, and FastAPI dependencies with clear lifecycle handling.

use_cases.title: Prepare Production Pooling
use_cases.description: Select pool sizes, enable connection checks, and add health endpoints for production services.

use_cases.title: Plan Safe Migrations
use_cases.description: Use Alembic patterns for schema changes, data migrations, rollback testing, and multi-database upgrades.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a SQLAlchemy 2.0 model for a customer record with typed columns and a relationship to orders.

prompt_templates.title: Add Async Sessions
prompt_templates.prompt: Show how to configure an async SQLAlchemy engine and per-request session dependency for a FastAPI service.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Transaction
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a funds transfer workflow using SQLAlchemy transactions and locking to prevent race conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Review Migration Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my plan for renaming a large production column with Alembic and suggest a low-downtime migration sequence.

output_examples.input: Create an async database setup for a web API.
output: Defines the async engine and session factory.
Adds a per-request dependency for session cleanup.
Notes driver and pool settings to verify before deployment.

output_examples.input: Help me plan an Alembic migration for a large table.
output: Splits the schema change into smaller migration steps.
Identifies when data backfill should run separately.
Suggests rollback checks and migration tests.
Warns about locking and transaction limits.

output_examples.input: Tune database pooling for a busy service.
output: Explains pool size, overflow, timeout, recycle, and pre-ping settings.
Relates pool choices to request concurrency and database capacity.

best_practices: Use SQLAlchemy 2.0 typed models with Mapped and mapped_column for clearer application contracts.
Manage sessions with context managers and explicit rollback paths around failed operations.
Test migrations up and down before applying them to shared or production databases.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode real database credentials in application code or migration files.
Do not rely on async lazy loading when eager loading is required for relationships.
Do not run destructive Alembic commands without checking the current target database.

faq.question: Which SQLAlchemy version does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets SQLAlchemy 2.0 style patterns with Python 3.10 or newer.

faq.question: Can this help with FastAPI services?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes async session dependency patterns that fit FastAPI request handling.

faq.question: Does the skill run database migrations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides migration guidance and commands, but users choose when to run them.

faq.question: Are the database URLs real credentials?
faq.answer: No. The URLs use placeholder values and must be replaced with secure configuration.

faq.question: Does it cover connection pooling?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains pool sizing, overflow, timeouts, health checks, and graceful shutdown.

faq.question: Can I use raw SQL with these patterns?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples prefer bound parameters when raw SQL is needed.
